mission.title: Install and Configure Microcks on Kubernetes
mission.description: Microcks is an open source, cloud native tool for API mocking and testing, allowing you to turn your API specifications into live mocks quickly. Installing Microcks enables you to streamline your API development and testing processes.

steps.title: Add the Microcks Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Microcks Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add microcks https://microcks.io/helm
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Microcks using Helm
steps.description:
Install Microcks in a new namespace called 'microcks' with the following command:
```bash
helm install microcks microcks/microcks --namespace microcks --create-namespace --version 1.13.2
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Microcks pods are running by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace microcks
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access the Microcks UI
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access the Microcks UI locally:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/microcks --namespace microcks 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Microcks will show the Microcks pods running in the 'microcks' namespace, and you will be able to access the Microcks UI at http://localhost:8080. Additionally, resource limits will be set for the Microcks deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add microcks https://microcks.io/helm
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Microcks release from the Kubernetes cluster with the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall microcks --namespace microcks
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Microcks:
```bash
kubectl delete crd microcks.microcks.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace microcks
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'microcks' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace microcks
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current Microcks configuration and resources:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace microcks -o yaml > microcks-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade Microcks to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade microcks microcks/microcks --namespace microcks --version 1.14.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Microcks pods are running and verify the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace microcks
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If Microcks pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace microcks
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be wrong.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the Microcks service is not reachable, verify that the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace microcks
```
Ensure that the service is correctly configured and that the port-forwarding command is still active.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being OOMKilled, check the resource usage:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace microcks
```
Consider increasing the memory limits in the deployment configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, ensure you are using the correct namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace microcks
```
If the release is not listed, it may need to be installed again.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have installed Helm and kubectl on your local machine.
